chunk_summary: Adolescent athletes require tailored macronutrient targets and meal timing strategies to support growth and training, with specific carbohydrate and protein guidelines based on activity levels and recovery needs.
learning_objectives: Understand the macronutrient requirements for adolescent athletes based on training intensity and duration.
Learn how to structure meals and snacks to optimize performance and recovery.
Identify practical food options to meet nutritional needs without relying on supplements.
Apply carbohydrate periodization and protein distribution strategies for adolescent athletes.
key_facts: Carbohydrate needs range from 3–12 g/kg/day depending on training intensity: 3–5 g/kg/day for light training, 8–12 g/kg/day for extreme endurance loads.
Pre-exercise carbohydrate intake: 1–4 g/kg 1–4 hours before activity; avoid fatty/high-fiber meals if GI symptoms occur.
During prolonged exercise (>1 hour), consume 30–60 g carbohydrate/hour; up to ~90 g/hour for events >2.5–3 hours.
Protein needs: 1.2–2.0 g/kg/day, distributed across meals (~0.3 g/kg after key sessions and every 3–5 hours).
Early recovery refueling: 1–1.2 g/kg/hour for the first 4 hours when <8 hours exist between hard sessions.
Indian foods like dal–rice, paneer, eggs, and milk-based snacks can meet macronutrient needs without supplements.
reference_quote: Sports nutrition for adolescents should be framed as 'supporting growth plus training.' Start by protecting meal structure (breakfast, lunch, dinner) and adding planned training snacks; then individualize macronutrient targets to training load, growth trajectory, and goals.
document_key_topics: Carbohydrate and protein guidelines for training and recovery
Practical food options for adolescent athletes
